title: Correlate k6 results with Grafana observability

blocks.content:
Welcome to the learning path that walks you from a Grafana Cloud k6 spike test through metrics, logs, and traces so you can judge whether a passing test really means the system is ready.

- Load generators answer a narrow question: did this script, from this network path, stay inside thresholds while you ramped load?
- Customers ask a wider one: did their requests stay healthy across regions, DNS, caches, and dependencies, and when something wobbled, what broke and where time went?
- When those answers disagree, you need one shared clock and a short walk through the rest of the stack.

- You play a performance engineer looking at quickpizza, a demo API your team spikes before launch. The scenario is intentionally small so you can finish in one sitting; the workflow transfers to any service you already instrument.

In one UTC window you combine:

- k6 for client-side timings and checks from the test runtime.
- Prometheus synthetic probes for a rough, multi-region view of the same endpoint from outside your VPC.
- Loki application logs for error class and whether errors cluster before, during, or after the VU peak.
- Tempo traces to see which spans paid the latency bill.

One signal type does not replace another. If you only read the k6 summary, you can still miss regional pain, slow recovery, or a dependency that does not show up as a failed check.

- Synthetic monitoring is usually a runtime concern, separate from a scripted load test. This path still opens probe metrics in Prometheus first because they answer a different question than k6 alone: during the same UTC window as your spike, what did small, steady external checks from multiple regions record?
- You are time-aligning two signals about one event so a passing generator view does not hide probe-side pain or slow recovery. You are not mixing performance testing and monitoring into one product.

You record one test start time in UTC and reuse that window in **Explore** for every signal type, in this order:

1. Prometheus: probe latency and recovery so you compare generator view with what the probes saw.
1. Loki: error lines and trends so you know whether the spike coincided with a new failure mode.
1. Tempo: error and slow spans so you connect user-visible latency to concrete work inside the service graph.

That order matches how many teams triage after a test: wide health, then error text, then fine-grained latency.

The image below shows an example Grafana Cloud k6 test overview you use as the anchor before you open **Explore**.

![Grafana Cloud k6 test overview showing performance trends for a run, the starting point before you correlate with metrics, logs, and traces in Explore](/media/docs/k6/screenshot-grafana-cloud-k6-test-overview-trends.png)

## Here's what to expect

When you complete this path, you'll be able to:

- Verify Grafana Cloud k6, `grafanacloud-k6`, Prometheus, Loki, and Tempo paths before you run a spike, then align generator and application metrics on one time range in **Explore**
- Run a controlled spike test in Grafana Cloud k6 with a live metrics view, review the **Test Result** view for thresholds and checks, and capture a UTC anchor time plus baseline numbers for analysis
- Compare k6 latency with synthetic probe metrics in Prometheus
- Classify errors and timing from Loki logs for the demo service
- Locate slow and error spans in Tempo and relate them to end-to-end latency
- Draft a short postmortem narrative that ties the signals together

## Before you begin

- You stay in Grafana Cloud k6 and **Explore** for one short spike and a single investigation window.
- This path does not teach long soak or capacity planning, recording rules, wiring new telemetry pipelines, running k6 from CI/CD, or SLO and error budget math. After you can run this loop manually, use other docs and learning paths for those topics. Full dashboard authoring stays out of scope; you only stage a short-lived layout for the spike.

Before you start, ensure you have:

- A Grafana Cloud account. To create one, refer to [Grafana Cloud](https://grafana.com/signup/cloud/connect-account/).
- Access to Grafana Cloud k6 and to **Explore** with the `grafanacloud-k6` data source plus Prometheus, Loki, and Tempo data sources connected (demo stacks often ship prefilled sources; names may differ from the examples in the steps).
- Enough familiarity to paste and run a small k6 script. If you need a primer first, complete [Run your first k6 performance test](/docs/learning-paths/run-first-k6-test/).
- Grafana Cloud quickpizza-style demos already emit application metrics, logs, and traces your administrators wire into those stores; the scenario milestone has you confirm label names before the spike. If your stack uses different service names or collectors, substitute the selectors your administrator documents.
- Optional: If your organization also sends k6 run metrics to Prometheus through remote write, you can graph those series beside probes in **Explore** in the same UTC window; this path does not configure remote write. Refer to [Prometheus remote write](/docs/k6/latest/results-output/real-time/prometheus-remote-write/) outside this lab.

## POV and trial readiness

Use this path during a product POV or trial when Grafana Cloud k6 and observability already reach the workload you test. The lab assumes you can query k6 results, probe or application metrics, logs, and traces in one Grafana stack. If a signal is missing on day one, close that gap first, then start the scenario milestone.

| If this is missing | Start here |
| --- | --- |
| No completed Grafana Cloud k6 run yet | [Run your first k6 performance test](/docs/learning-paths/run-first-k6-test/) |
| No traces in Tempo for the service under test | [Send traces to Grafana Cloud using Alloy](/docs/learning-paths/send-traces-alloy/) |
| No application logs in Loki | [Send logs to Grafana Cloud using Alloy](/docs/learning-paths/send-logs-alloy-loki/) |
| No synthetic probe metrics in Prometheus | [Detect customer-visible outages using Synthetic Monitoring](/docs/learning-paths/detect-outages-synthetic-monitoring/) |
| Stakeholders expect a saved dashboard, not only **Explore** | [Correlate results in Grafana](/docs/grafana-cloud/testing/k6/analyze-results/correlate-results-in-grafana/) and the optional dashboard steps in the **Prepare a live metrics view** milestone |

For a typical POV narrative, you run the spike in Grafana Cloud k6, watch load on a dashboard or **Explore** layout, then walk the same UTC window through Prometheus, Loki, and Tempo. That sequence matches what teams ask for when they compare Grafana Cloud to siloed load tools.


## Troubleshooting

If you get stuck, we've got your back! Where appropriate, troubleshooting information is just a click away.

## More to explore

We understand you might want to explore other capabilities not strictly on this path. We'll provide you opportunities where it makes sense.
